name: Stop the Steal
description: Stop the Steal is a conspiracy theory asserting that widespread voter fraud occurred in the 2020 United States presidential election, resulting in Donald Trump's defeat. The theory alleges various forms of electoral misconduct, including manipulated voting machines, fraudulent ballots, and illegal vote counting. Originating prior to the election, the narrative gained significant traction following Trump's loss, fueled by his own pronouncements and amplified through social media. Proponents cite statistical anomalies, anecdotal accounts of irregularities, and alleged expert analyses of voting data as evidence. Mainstream scholarly perspectives and official investigations, including multiple recounts and audits, have consistently refuted these claims, finding no evidence of widespread fraud sufficient to alter the election outcome. Legal challenges brought by Trump and his allies were overwhelmingly dismissed by courts, including the Supreme Court. The "Stop the Steal" narrative has been linked to the January 6, 2021 attack on the U.S. Capitol, with participants citing it as a motivating factor. The theory continues to be influential within certain segments of the American population, contributing to political polarization and distrust in democratic institutions. Despite the lack of credible evidence, it persists as a significant belief system influencing political discourse and action.
subtitle: Disputed claims of 2020 election fraud.

claims.claim: Voting machines were manipulated to switch votes from Trump to Biden.
claims.evidence: Affidavits from poll watchers, alleged statistical anomalies in vote counts, and claims about the Dominion Voting Systems software.
claims.criticism: Forensic audits and investigations by cybersecurity experts and government agencies found no evidence of widespread manipulation of voting machines. Allegations regarding Dominion Voting Systems have been widely debunked and led to defamation lawsuits.

claims.claim: Widespread fraudulent ballots were submitted and counted.
claims.evidence: Allegations of dead people voting, multiple votes being cast by the same individuals, and ballots being illegally manufactured or altered.
claims.criticism: Official investigations and audits have found isolated instances of voter fraud, but not on a scale large enough to affect the election outcome. Claims of widespread fraud have been based on misinformation and misinterpretations of data.

claims.claim: Illegal vote counting occurred, including observers being denied access and ballots being counted in secret.
claims.evidence: Affidavits from poll watchers alleging they were prevented from observing the vote counting process and claims about irregularities in the handling of ballots.
claims.criticism: Courts have reviewed these claims and found them to be without merit or insufficient to justify overturning election results. Claims of secret counting often stem from standard procedures for processing absentee ballots.

keyFigures.name: Donald Trump
keyFigures.role: Promoted the Stop the Steal narrative and challenged the election results.

keyFigures.name: Rudy Giuliani
keyFigures.role: Served as Trump's lawyer and publicly promoted unsubstantiated claims of voter fraud.

keyFigures.name: Sidney Powell
keyFigures.role: Attorney who filed lawsuits alleging widespread voter fraud and promoted conspiracy theories.

keyFigures.name: Christopher Krebs
keyFigures.role: Director of the Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) who affirmed the security of the 2020 election and was subsequently fired.

keyFigures.name: Bill Barr
keyFigures.role: Attorney General who stated the Justice Department found no evidence of widespread voter fraud that could change the election outcome.

mythologyConnections: The idea of a 'stolen election' can be seen as a modern parallel to myths of betrayal and usurpation of power.
The narrative taps into anxieties about societal decline and the loss of traditional values, common themes in mythology.

timeline.event: Major news outlets project Joe Biden as the winner of the election.
Trump and his allies file numerous lawsuits challenging the election results, most of which are dismissed.
Attack on the U.S. Capitol by supporters of Trump seeking to overturn the election results.

sources.author: U.S. Department of Justice
sources.work: Statements and reports on election fraud investigations
sources.title: U.S. Department of Justice

sources.author: Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA)
sources.work: Statements and reports on the security of the 2020 election
sources.title: Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA)
